3A_Bibliographic.citation: Sampasa-Kanyinga, H., Colman, I., Goldfield, G. S., Janssen, I., Wang, J., Tremblay, M. S., Barnes, J. D., Walsh, J. J., & Chaput, J.-P. (2021). 24-Hour movement behaviors and internalizing and externalizing behaviors among youth. Journal of Adolescent Health, 68(5), 969-977.

3C_Exposure.exposure_measure: Meeting Canadian 24-Hour Movement Guidelines: screen time component is recreational screen time <=2 hours/day (also physical activity >=60 min/day for 7 days, sleep 9-11 hours/night)
modalities_included: total recreational screen time (TV, video watching, video games, texting, social media, video chat)
3C_Exposure.how_operationalized: Screen time: Youth Screen Time Survey, weighted average of weekday/weekend across 6 modalities. Binary: meeting <=2 hrs/day recommendation. Physical activity: Youth Risk Behavior Survey single item (days active >=60 min in past 7 days; meeting = 7 days). Sleep: Parent Sleep Disturbance Scale (meeting = 9-11 hrs/night). Eight combinations of meeting/not meeting each guideline.

3D_Outcome.measure_used: Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL/6-18), parent-report. Raw scores for total problems (all 8 syndrome scales), internalizing (anxious/depressed, withdrawn/depressed, somatic complaints), externalizing (rule-breaking, aggressive behavior).
3D_Outcome.base_rate_if_binary: N/A - continuous count outcomes. Mean total problems: 18.2 (SD=18.0); internalizing: 5.0 (SD=5.5); externalizing: 4.2 (SD=5.5)

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Negative binomial regression for overdispersed count outcomes. Adjusted for cluster effect of 21 sites using svyset. Prevalence ratios (PR) reported. Gender x meeting guidelines interaction tested (not significant). Complete case analysis.
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, gender, ethnicity (African American, Asian, Caucasian, Hispanic, multiracial), family income (1-10 scale), parental education (years), BMI z-scores
3E_Design_Analysis.SES_in_model: yes - family income and parental education were covariates in the multivariable models
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: negative binomial regression analyses were used to examine the associations of meeting different combinations of movement behavior recommendations with the three outcomes. Prevalence ratios (PR), their 95% confidence intervals (CIs), and p-values are presented.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Meeting all 3 guidelines (PA+screen+sleep) vs none -> total problems
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: The prevalence ratio of the group meeting all three recommendations was .77 (95% confidence interval [CI]: .68-.86) for total problem scores
3F_Effect_Sizes.below_threshold: no (PR 0.77 equivalent to 23% reduction, outside 0.80-1.20 range)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Meeting all 3 guidelines vs none -> internalizing problems
Meeting all 3 guidelines vs none -> externalizing problems
Meeting screen time only vs none -> total problems
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Only screen time [total problems multivariable]: PR .89 (95% CI .82-.97)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Meeting screen time only vs none -> internalizing problems (not significant)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Only screen time [internalizing multivariable]: PR .96 (95% CI .88-1.06)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Meeting screen time only vs none -> externalizing problems
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Only screen time [externalizing multivariable]: PR .85 (95% CI .77-.94)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Meeting screen time + sleep vs none -> total problems
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Screen time + sleep duration [total problems multivariable]: PR .76 (95% CI .71-.82)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Meeting screen time + sleep vs none -> internalizing problems
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Screen time + sleep duration [internalizing multivariable]: PR .83 (95% CI .77-.89)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Meeting screen time + sleep vs none -> externalizing problems
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Screen time + sleep duration [externalizing multivariable]: PR .74 (95% CI .67-.81)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Meeting physical activity only vs none -> total problems (not significant)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Only physical activity [total problems multivariable]: PR 1.06 (95% CI .99-1.15)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Meeting physical activity only vs none -> externalizing problems (HIGHER problems, counterintuitive)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: youth who met the physical activity recommendation had a greater risk of externalizing problems than those who met none of the recommendations (PR: 1.18; 95% CI: 1.08-1.30)

3G_Interpretation.verbatim_recommendation: These findings can help to inform the development of early intervention efforts directed towards the improvement of the emotional and behavioral health of youth. Increased awareness is needed among all stakeholders, including parents, caregivers, health professionals, policymakers, and youth themselves on the benefits of meeting the 24-hour movement guidelines.

3H_Rhetorical.pattern_detail: Frames screen time and sleep as modifiable factors within the 24-hour movement guidelines framework. Key finding is that screen time + sleep recommendations drive the associations more than physical activity. Prevalence ratios of 0.77-0.79 for meeting all 3 guidelines represent moderate effects. Cross-sectional design acknowledged as limitation.
3H_Rhetorical.list_1_qualifier: yes - 'The results from these cross-sectional analyses should not be interpreted as being causal, but rather informative for future hypothesis generation'

3I_Flags.ambiguity_notes: Cross-sectional design limits causal inference. Screen time is operationalized as binary (meeting <=2 hrs/day or not), which collapses substantial variation. Physical activity measured with single item (weak measure acknowledged by authors). 35.1% met screen time guideline; only 3.9% met all three. The screen time-specific effects (PR 0.85-0.96) are modest; the stronger effects emerge from screen time + sleep combinations (PR 0.74-0.83). Cannot disentangle screen time effects from sleep effects in the combined measures.
